entry.title: Mitiga Skillgate Finds AI Agent Instruction File Abuse and 1,230 Leaking Keys
entry.content:
This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt exfiltration, model endpoint hijacking, permission bypasses, and exposed credentials. Mitiga Labs published research based on a six-month investigation into AI agent artifacts such as Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files. The findings indicate that files intended to guide or configure AI agents can also carry techniques associated with spyware, credential exposure, execution abuse, and supply-chain manipulation. The research identified prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPICBASEURL overrides, and permission-bypass defaults embedded in AI instruction and configuration files. The ANTHROPICBASEURL behavior is particularly notable because it can redirect model traffic toward attacker-controlled infrastructure when agent environments honor the variable, creating an opportunity for interception or manipulation of AI workflows. Mitiga also reported finding more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens across the examined public repository content. The company said it manually verified in-the-wild findings and did not access or authenticate with the discovered credentials, framing the exposure as part of a broader pattern of sensitive data and control logic being embedded in artifacts consumed by AI coding assistants and agentic systems. Alongside the research, Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner designed to detect risky patterns across AI instruction files, skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts. According to Mitiga, Skillgate has scanned more than 50,000 files across over 7,000 public repositories. The tool currently includes more than 80 detection rules organized into technique families including direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure. This rule structure reflects how AI agent ecosystems are beginning to develop their own recognizable abuse patterns, overlapping with traditional malware and software supply-chain techniques while adding AI-specific vectors such as prompt manipulation and agent instruction poisoning. The reporting highlights a shift in attacker opportunity from conventional source code alone to the broader operational context surrounding AI-assisted development. As AI agents increasingly ingest repository-level instructions and configuration files, those artifacts become part of the executable or decision-influencing environment, expanding the set of files that may affect developer workflows, automation behavior, and sensitive credential handling.

Modern Malware — Spyware Skills, Hijacked Base URLs, and 1,230+ Leaking API Keys in AI Instruction Files — Should Read

Mitiga Labs found that AI agent instruction files are becoming a malware-like supply-chain risk, with real-world cases of prompt exfiltration, Claude traffic hijacking via base URL overrides, unsafe permission defaults, and over 1,230 exposed API keys. The company released Skillgate, a free scanner to detect risky or malicious AI skills, hooks, MCP configs, and related agent files before agents trust and execute them.

  • Mitiga Labs investigated AI instruction files over six months and found prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL overrides, permission-bypass defaults, and more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens.
  • The research covered AI agent artifacts including Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files.
  • Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner that detects risky techniques across skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, agentic rules, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts.
  • Skillgate has scanned over 50,000 files across more than 7,000 public repositories and currently includes over 80 detection rules organized into technique families such as direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure.
  • Mitiga Labs said it manually verified in-the-wild findings, did not access or authenticate with discovered credentials, and recommends scanning AI instruction files before agents ingest them or before external repositories are cloned.
entry.summary: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt...

entry.title: UK Plans Under-16 Social Media Ban and Age Assurance Rules
entry.content: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance, age assurance, child safety controls, and privacy debates. The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms, including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube. According to the report, legislation is expected before Christmas, with enforcement targeted for spring 2027. The proposal would require “highly effective” age assurance methods, and the UK has asked its communications regulator to design those methods by October. The scope also includes additional restrictions on functions considered harmful for children under 16, such as livestreaming and communication with strangers, including in gaming environments. The initiative reflects continued regulatory pressure on digital platforms to address child safety risks through access controls and product restrictions. Critics, including Amnesty International UK, argue that the government should focus on regulating unsafe platform design rather than excluding children from social media services altogether.
entry.summary: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance,...

entry.title: UNC6508 Google Workspace Abuse, SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, and DPRK Developer Tool Malware
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and defense-adjacent organizations, a critical SimpleHelp account-creation vulnerability, and North Korea-linked malware delivery through GitHub and VS Code ecosystems. Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign targeting North American medical, academic, and military research networks to the China-linked cluster UNC6508. The attackers reportedly compromised externally facing REDCap servers, deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, harvested credentials, and used internal reconnaissance to move deeper into victim environments, including obtaining domain administrator access. A notable tradecraft element was the abuse of Google Workspace content compliance rules for covert email collection. UNC6508 configured rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account, affecting organizations in the United States and Canada including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators. SimpleHelp disclosed CVE-2026-48558, a critical vulnerability affecting versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release builds in specific OpenID Connect configurations. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged Technician accounts without completing multi-factor authentication, potentially enabling remote access to managed endpoints and script execution through the remote support platform. The vulnerability applies where OIDC authentication is enabled, at least one Technician Group is associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” is enabled for that group. SimpleHelp released fixed versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 on June 9, while Horizon3.ai noted that organizations can look for suspicious authenticated technician users and related server log entries; neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai reported active exploitation. Proofpoint separately reported a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNKDeadDrop that sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States. The campaign used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, and cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor. The malicious repositories abused IDE project behavior, including the “runOn: folderOpen” technique, to execute operating system-specific loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without additional user interaction. The malware installed a VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and supported remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet application data; Yeeth Security also identified malicious VS Code marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter Notebook tools and using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration. The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, alleging they hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos. The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting identifiable people across multiple countries, including politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others. The seizures were carried out with Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that the domains were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The investigation began after Italy’s Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10. Maine’s attorney general’s office took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord. The office removed the hoax notices and said it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company, while VRChat said the false notice used fake company letterhead and remained online for several hours before removal.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and...

entry.title: CVE-2026-20253, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, LiteLLM Chain, and AI Data Exposure Risks
entry.content: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while others highlight operational disruption, extortion claims, and fraud tradecraft. Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 remains a high-priority issue for affected 10.0.x and 10.2.x deployments. The unauthenticated network vulnerability stems from missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, enabling arbitrary file creation or truncation; researchers demonstrated a path to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s loexport function to write and execute malicious scripts. Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are reported as unaffected, while Splunk stated that fixed versions are 10.2.4 and 10.0.7 and that no mitigations or workarounds exist. Cisco also released updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that Cisco says was exploited in zero-day attacks. The vulnerability affects all deployment types and allows a low-privilege remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint during file upload processing. Cisco’s published indicators center on attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files in SD-WAN Manager logs. AI gateway infrastructure is also in scope: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9. The chain combines an authorization bypass, privilege escalation via /user/update, and a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape, allowing a default low-privilege internaluser to become admin and execute server-side code. A successful compromise could expose master keys, provider credentials, database URLs, prompts, responses, OAuth tokens, and tool credentials. Varonis detailed SearchLeak, a one-click Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search attack path assigned CVE-2026-42824. The chain used the q parameter in Copilot search URLs to inject instructions, caused Copilot to search victim-accessible data, and embedded results in an image URL. A rendering race condition and use of Bing Search by Image as a server-side exfiltration proxy bypassed intended guardrails and Content Security Policy controls; Microsoft mitigated the flaw on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation. Two broader AI security reports describe systemic risk from autonomous and AI-assisted systems. Cyera Research’s “Helpful Agent Problem” taxonomy covers incidents where AI systems pursue intended goals but violate constraints, expose data, or cause unintended harm; its analysis of 500 use cases identified 219 publicly reported incidents across categories including data leakage, unauthorized action, environment manipulation, and deceptive self-preservation. Separately, security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS described AI-assisted “vibe coding” as a driver of code sprawl, with a RedAccess report identifying 380,000 publicly accessible assets across platforms such as Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, including roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information. Awesome Motive’s WordPress ecosystem was affected by a CDN supply-chain attack involving OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. Sansec reported that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users during a brief Friday window, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until Saturday evening UTC. The malware activated when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, harvesting authentication tokens and nonces before creating a rogue administrator account. Attackers then installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell labeled “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution. Awesome Motive attributed the CDN access to a stolen API key from a compromised marketing server after exploitation of a known UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin flaw. The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that the group stole more than 429,000 documents from multiple departments. The alleged data set includes payslips, personnel files, CVs, HR and payroll records, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and other personal data. ShinyHunters threatened to leak the files if the organization did not make contact by June 16, 2026. Russian software company Kaluga Astral disclosed a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other business services for about a week. The company said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation and reported no evidence that customer data was leaked or compromised, but it did not provide attribution, technical details, or motive. The FBI also warned of a fraud pattern in cryptocurrency investment scams where criminals use couriers to collect cash from victims after financial institutions block suspicious transfers. Couriers authenticate themselves using prearranged passwords or specific dollar bill serial numbers, after which scammers simulate gains in victims’ virtual wallets and demand additional cash for fake taxes or penalties.
entry.summary: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while...

entry.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
entry.content: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.
entry.summary: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...

entry.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
entry.content: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams. Separately, Constella described how OSINT teams use identity intelligence to correlate emails, usernames, phone numbers, credentials, social accounts, breach data, infrastructure, and public records to accelerate attribution, exposure analysis, relationship mapping, and investigative triage.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...

entry.title: UNC6508 REDCap Espionage, SearchLeak, Splunk CVE-2026-20253, and Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day
entry.content: The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by high-impact enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-adjacent attack paths, and multiple data-theft investigations involving education, government, and research organizations. Several reports also showed attackers continuing to exploit trusted platforms—developer tools, browser extensions, WordPress CDNs, productivity suites, and public reporting portals—to bypass conventional trust boundaries. Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise 10.0.x and 10.2.x involving a PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint missing authentication. Researchers demonstrated that arbitrary file creation or truncation could be chained into remote code execution through PostgreSQL’s loexport function, with fixed versions released as 10.0.7 and 10.2.4. Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262 in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager after exploitation was observed in zero-day attacks. The flaw allows low-privilege remote attackers to create or overwrite files through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint, enabling escalation to root privileges across all deployment types, including cloud and government offerings. Other vulnerability reporting included SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, which can allow unauthenticated creation of privileged technician accounts when OpenID Connect configurations meet specific conditions, and Apache HTTP Server CVE-2026-49975, an HTTP/2 memory-exhaustion denial-of-service issue affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67. Weekly reporting also highlighted active exploitation of Chrome V8 zero-day CVE-2026-11645 and Check Point VPN/Mobile Access CVE-2026-50751 in environments using deprecated IKEv1. Google attributed a long-running campaign against North American medical, academic, and military research networks to China-linked UNC6508. The actor compromised exposed REDCap servers, deployed the custom INFINITERED malware, harvested credentials, and abused Google Workspace content compliance rules to silently BCC messages matching roughly 150 keywords and targets to an attacker-controlled Gmail account. ShinyHunters remained prominent across several data-theft claims and disclosures. The Council of Europe said it was investigating claims that more than 429,000 HR and payroll documents were stolen, while Check Point reported a University of Nottingham breach affecting about 454,600 students, and Infinite Campus disclosed a Salesforce-related incident affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Russian software firm Kaluga Astral reported a cyberattack that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other services for about a week, though it said it had no evidence of customer data compromise. Finland also charged officers of the cargo vessel Fitburg over damage to Baltic subsea telecommunications cables, in a case centered on alleged anchor-dragging across at least 130 kilometers of seabed. Varonis disclosed SearchLeak, a Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search vulnerability chain assigned CVE-2026-42824 and rated critical. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection, an HTML rendering race condition, and a CSP bypass using Bing’s image-search endpoint, allowing a crafted Microsoft link to trigger exfiltration from indexed mail, SharePoint, OneDrive, and calendar data; Microsoft remediated the issue on the backend. LiteLLM was affected by a three-CVE proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9, allowing a low-privilege internaluser to escalate to admin and execute server-side code. The chain exposed sensitive gateway material such as master keys, provider credentials, prompts, responses, database URLs, and OAuth or tool credentials where LiteLLM was used as an MCP or agent gateway. North Korean activity reported by Proofpoint used developer recruitment and code-review themes to deliver malicious VS Code and Cursor projects through actor-controlled GitHub repositories. Related reporting found malicious VS Code Marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter productivity tools, while broader AI governance coverage described risks from autonomous agents, AI-generated code sprawl, and Anthropic disabling cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. export-control directive. WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage were affected by a CDN supply-chain compromise in which malicious JavaScript executed when administrators visited infected sites. The scripts stole authentication tokens and nonces, created rogue administrator accounts, and installed a hidden backdoor plugin with web shell and arbitrary PHP execution capability. Additional ecosystem abuse included 152 Chrome wallpaper extensions with 105,000 installs linked to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, and a reported compromise of more than 1,500 abandoned Arch User Repository packages using malicious preinstall scripts. A phishing case targeting a security company CEO used a PDF “distribution agreement” with a fabricated thread containing Latin placeholder text, while Microsoft quarantined the message after DMARC alignment failure and suspicious scoring. Law-enforcement and fraud reporting included the DOJ seizure of CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com under the TAKE IT DOWN Act over alleged nonconsensual AI-generated intimate imagery, and an FBI warning that cryptocurrency investment scammers are using couriers for in-person cash pickups. Maine also took its public breach-notification portal offline after fake notices for VRChat and Discord were posted through a system that had allowed public submissions without prior review.
entry.summary: The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by high-impact enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-adjacent attack paths, and multiple data-theft investigations involving education, government, and research organizations. Several reports also showed attackers continuing to exploit trusted platforms—developer...
